name: Udayana
dates_notes: Internal date of 906 Śaka (= 984 CE) in the Lakṣaṇāvalī gives a firm composition-anchor; Mishra (1936) and Matilal (1977) date Udayana's active period to c. 975–1050 CE. Mithilā Naiyāyika; predates and is foundational to Gaṅgeśa's Navya-Nyāya.
core_thesis: Udayana is the figure in whom Old-Nyāya reaches its constructive maturity and the figure to whom Gaṅgeśa's Navya-Nyāya is the immediate downstream. Three works fix the position. The *Nyāya-Kusumāñjali* offers the school's most developed *anumāna* for *īśvara*: five concentric arguments — *kāryāt* (from effects requiring an intelligent agent), *āyojanāt* (from coordinated complexity), *dhṛteḥ* (from cosmic maintenance), *padāt* (from the meaningfulness of language requiring a divine *saṅketa*), *pratyayataḥ* (from the authority of *śruti* requiring a reliable speaker). The argument is meticulously *vyāpti*-formal and survives as the standard Naiyāyika *īśvara-sādhana*. The *Ātma-Tattva-Viveka* prosecutes a sustained refutation of Buddhist *kṣaṇika-vāda* and *nairātmya-vāda* by demonstrating that the unity-of-cognising-subject across remembered, anticipated, and currently perceived contents requires an enduring *ātman*; the four-chapter structure attacks four successive Buddhist positions. The *Tātparya-Pariśuddhi* sub-commentates Vāchaspati Miśra's *Tātparya-Ṭīkā* on Uddyotakara's *Vārttika* on Vātsyāyana's *Bhāṣya* on Akṣapāda's *Sūtra* — five-tier scholasticism — and is the form in which the entire Old-Nyāya stack is read in late-medieval pāṭhaśālās. For Vedānta, Udayana is a critical interlocutor on three counts. The *īśvara-anumāna* is engaged by Śrīharṣa's *Khaṇḍana-Khaṇḍa-Khādya* in negative dialectic and by Madhva's school in defence; the *ātman*-realism is engaged by Advaita and accepted with modification by Madhva and Rāmānuja; the perceptual-realism is the Naiyāyika position that Citsukha and Madhusūdana explicitly target.

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Kusumanjali
engaged_works.composition_dates_notes: Composed in Udayana's mature period; the Lakṣaṇāvalī's 984 CE colophon anchors the relative chronology.
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored independent treatise on natural theology; widely accepted as the canonical Naiyāyika īśvara-proof.
engaged_works.summary: Five-*stabaka* (chapter) treatise demonstrating *īśvara* by formal *anumāna*. Each *stabaka* prosecutes one of the five proofs: *kāryāt*, *āyojanāt*, *dhṛteḥ*, *padāt*, *pratyayataḥ*. The architecture is dialectical: each chapter opens with the *pūrvapakṣa* — Mīmāṃsā denial that *īśvara* is needed for any of the *vedānta*-school explanatory tasks, Buddhist denial of intelligent cosmic-cause talk, Cārvāka denial of the entire framework — and concludes with the *uttarapakṣa* refining the *anumāna*. The *kāryāt* argument is the most exhaustively treated: the *vyāpti* between *kārya-tva* (being-an-effect) and *buddhimat-kāraṇa-janya-tva* (being-produced-by-an-intelligent-cause) is defended against every standard Mīmāṃsā and Buddhist *upādhi*-objection. The *padāt* argument turns on the necessity of a divine *saṅketa-kartṛ* for the original word-meaning convention, anticipating the Bhartṛhari-influenced Mīmāṃsā position that *vāc* is *anādi*. The *pratyayataḥ* argument grounds the authority of *Veda* in *īśvara* as *āpta* speaker — a direct rebuttal of Mīmāṃsā *apauruṣeyatva*. The treatise becomes the standard medieval Naiyāyika natural theology and is the target of Śrīharṣa's *Khaṇḍana* in late-twelfth-century Advaita negative dialectic.

engaged_works.title: Atma-Tattva-Viveka
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored polemical treatise against Buddhist no-self positions.
engaged_works.summary: Four-chapter polemical treatise refuting four Buddhist positions opposing the *ātman*-realist Naiyāyika thesis. Chapter 1 attacks *kṣaṇika-vāda*: instantaneous-being is shown to be incompatible with the unity of recognising-subject across temporally distinct cognitions, and the standard Sautrāntika rejoinder via *santāna* (causal series) is shown to fail under analysis of *pratyabhijñā* (recognition). Chapter 2 attacks *bāhyārtha-bhaṅga* (the denial of extra-mental objects): perceptual cognition is argued to require an extra-mental cause, and the Yogācāra appeal to *vāsanā*-residue is challenged on the grounds that the *vāsanā*-bearer cannot itself be momentary. Chapter 3 attacks *vijñāna-mātra* (consciousness-only): *cit*-only ontology is argued to be incoherent on its own terms because *artha*- and *jñāna*-distinction is intra-cognitive but not reducible to *cit*. Chapter 4 attacks *śūnya-vāda*: the analysis of *anyathā-khyāti* (cognition mistaking one thing for another) is argued to require both terms of the misidentification to be real, refuting any radical-emptiness reading. The treatise's enduring importance is methodological: it is the model for late-medieval Indian polemical-treatise construction, including in Advaita (Citsukha) and Dvaita (Vyāsatīrtha).

engaged_works.title: Nyaya-Varttika-Tatparya-Parisuddhi
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Vāchaspati Miśra's Tātparya-Ṭīkā; the apex of pre-Gaṅgeśa Naiyāyika scholasticism.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on Vāchaspati Miśra's *Nyāya-Vārttika-Tātparya-Ṭīkā*, completing the five-tier Old-Nyāya stack: *Sūtra* (Akṣapāda) → *Bhāṣya* (Vātsyāyana) → *Vārttika* (Uddyotakara) → *Tātparya-Ṭīkā* (Vāchaspati Miśra) → *Pariśuddhi* (Udayana). The *Pariśuddhi* refines the *pramāṇa*-doctrine inherited from the four prior layers, polishes the analysis of *vyāpti* and *upādhi*, and consolidates the *jāti*-realism against persisting Buddhist nominalist pressure. Two contributions are systemically important. First, the analysis of *paryāpti* — the conditions under which a property's being-located-in something exhausts the relevant predication — anticipates Gaṅgeśa's later technical apparatus by a century. Second, the analysis of *abhāva*-pratiyogitva* — the relation between an absence and its absentee — is given the treatment that Gaṅgeśa's *Tattva-Cintāmaṇi* presupposes. The *Pariśuddhi* is the form in which the entire Old-Nyāya stack is read in late-medieval Mithilā and Navadvīpa pāṭhaśālās; without it, Gaṅgeśa is unintelligible.
